findings.title: singlePayload dead code with void suppression
findings.why_it_matters: singlePayload is declared but never used — the `void singlePayload` pattern is a code smell indicating a variable was created then immediately discarded. It adds noise and confusion for future maintainers.
evidence: Line 279: `const singlePayload = [payload]` — declared but only used on line 289 as `void singlePayload`
The variable is not passed to _handleBatchError or used anywhere else in the block
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `const singlePayload = [payload]` and `void singlePayload` lines entirely — they serve no purpose.

findings.title: Monotonic RPC loop returns true even on per-record errors
findings.why_it_matters: In the monotonic RPC path, when a per-record RPC call fails, `_handleBatchError` is called and execution `continue`s. After the loop, the function returns `true` (line 293). But _uploadBatch is supposed to return false on error. The caller (`_doUpload`) doesn't use the return value for anything critical in the current implementation, but the contract is misleading — a partially-failed batch is reported as success.
evidence: Lines 277-292: per-record RPC failure calls _handleBatchError then `continue` — the failed entry is handled (retry scheduled or dead-lettered)
Line 293: `return true` always executes after the loop, even if some records failed
The return value of _uploadBatch is not used by _doUpload (checked) — so this is a semantic/future-proofing concern rather than an active bug
findings.suggested_fix: Track a `allSucceeded` boolean in the loop. Set to false when any record fails. Return `allSucceeded` instead of `true`. This makes the partial-failure case explicit.

findings.title: No test for delete operation queue entries
findings.why_it_matters: The syncEngine processes SyncQueueEntry.operation values of 'put', 'add', and 'delete'. All test fixtures use 'put'. The delete operation payload is `{ id: recordId }` and should be upserted/inserted like any other — but this is not verified. A delete payload upserted to Supabase would incorrectly set all non-id columns to null depending on the table's conflict strategy.
evidence: makeEntry() always creates operation: 'put' entries (line 112 of test file)
No test exercises operation: 'delete' through the upload path
SyncQueueEntry interface has operation: 'put' | 'add' | 'delete' — the upload engine processes all three identically (by payload) but this is not explicitly tested
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test: `makeEntry({ operation: 'delete', payload: { id: 'rec-1' } })` → upsert called with `{ id: 'rec-1' }` as the payload.

findings.title: makeEntry uses Math.random() for id — non-deterministic IDs in tests
findings.why_it_matters: Using `Math.floor(Math.random() * 10000) + 1` for test entry IDs can produce collisions in tests that create multiple entries with the same expected id, making test assertions brittle. More importantly, the Knowlune test patterns require deterministic test data.
evidence: Line 107: `id: Math.floor(Math.random() * 10000) + 1` — random ids can collide in theory, and test output is non-deterministic
Tests that assert specific id values (e.g., `expect(mockBulkDelete).toHaveBeenCalledWith([1])`) must set id explicitly in overrides — these work fine, but the default is misleading
findings.suggested_fix: Use a deterministic counter: `let _idCounter = 1; function makeEntry(...) { return { id: _idCounter++, ... } }`. Reset in beforeEach.

findings.title: Comment doc says pure module but now imports Dexie and Supabase
findings.why_it_matters: The header comment says 'Pure module — no React imports. Safe to import anywhere.' This is true (no React), but the comment omits that the module now imports Dexie and Supabase. The S04 stub said 'no Dexie, Zustand, or React imports' — that was a key contract for circular-import safety. Future readers may not realize this module is no longer side-effect-free.
evidence: Line 16: `Pure module — no React imports. Safe to import anywhere.`
The original S04 comment explicitly said 'no Dexie, Zustand, or React imports' — the S05 comment silently dropped the Dexie/Supabase part
findings.suggested_fix: Update the comment: 'Pure module — no React or Zustand imports. Imports Dexie (db) and Supabase client for upload. Safe to import in any non-React context.'

requirements.R1: met — queue entries deleted on success
requirements.R5: met — monotonic P0 tables → rpc() per record
requirements.R13: mostly met — 34 tests covering key scenarios; delete operation path not explicitly tested (see P2 finding)

residual_risks: Retry timer accumulation: if the engine never drains (e.g. constant 5xx), multiple setTimeout callbacks accumulate in the event loop. The plan acknowledges this and notes the dead-letter cap prevents infinite growth — acceptable for S05.

navigator.locks is spec'd as async (returns a Promise), but the fallback _uploadInFlight is synchronous — in multi-threaded environments (SharedWorkers) the boolean guard is not sufficient. Acceptable for S05 scope (main-thread only).
The monotonic RPC path returns true even when individual records fail — this is a minor semantic issue for the current caller (which ignores the return value) but could mislead future callers.
testing_gaps: delete operation through upload path not tested
skipSync: true table is not tested (tableEntry.skipSync check at line ~373)
multiple table names in same upload cycle not tested (only single-table scenarios)
verdict: Ready with fixes — 1 P2 (semantic), 4 P3 (cleanup). No blockers. All requirements met.
